result:
I’d dissolve the membrane between “us” and “them”—the invisible border that lets a human shrug at another’s pain because the other is the wrong nation, class, tribe, species, or century. One stroke: every mind would momentarily wear every other mind’s entire sensory history—pleasure, grief, exhaustion, elation—like a second skin. No explanation, no consent form, no filter. You would survive it, but you would not remain the same.

After that, the phrase “I don’t care” would sound like “I don’t breathe”—technically possible, but freakish, labor-intensive, and quickly self-punishing. Markets, religions, algorithms, and dinner-table jokes would reorganize overnight around the simple, unbearable knowledge that the distance between any two hearts is zero.

No utopia promised; people would still quarrel, still compete. But every war, every landfill, every law written to hoard fortune at the expense of strangers would now feel like cutting off your own legs in advance of a marathon. The world would keep its scars, yet stop picking them open.

That is the change I would make: a forced, merciful intimacy—brief as lightning, permanent as nerve damage—so that indifference becomes the one extinct species.

No utopia promised; people would still quarrel, still compete. But every war, every landfill, every law written to hoard fortune at the expense of strangers would now feel like cutting off your own legs in advance of a marathon. The world would keep its scars, yet stop picking them open.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
